title: SEGMENT 17 — PacBio + Illumina hybrid assemblies: historical workflow, less common now

lines.text: SEGMENT 17 — PacBio + Illumina hybrid assemblies: historical workflow, less common now

A brief segment on a workflow that was the dominant long-read approach for several years and has become less common in the HiFi era: hybrid assembly combining PacBio long reads with Illumina short reads.
The motivation was straightforward.
In the CLR era — when PacBio raw reads were at 85-90% accuracy — you couldn't assemble a genome from PacBio alone, because the per-base errors would propagate into the assembly.

The standard fix was to use PacBio long reads to determine the overall structure of the assembly — the contigs, the scaffolds, the spanning of repeats — and then use Illumina short reads, with their per-base accuracy, to polish the assembly: correct individual base errors in the PacBio-derived sequence.

The workflow looked like: sequence the genome with PacBio CLR to about thirty- or fifty-x coverage, assemble using one of the long-read assemblers (Canu, Flye, Falcon, hifiasm in later versions), then sequence the same genome with Illumina to about thirty-x coverage and use the Illumina reads to polish the PacBio assembly with tools like Pilon, racon, or medaka.
The output was a genome assembly with PacBio-derived structure and Illumina-derived per-base accuracy.
The cost: two separate sequencing runs on two platforms.
In the HiFi era, this is largely obsolete.
HiFi reads are already Q30-Q40 accurate, so the Illumina polishing step is no longer needed.
A standard HiFi assembly today uses HiFi reads only — assembled with a tool like hifiasm — and the resulting assembly is both highly contiguous and highly accurate without any short-read polishing.
The workflow has simplified, and the per-genome cost has dropped because you don't need a second platform.
Hybrid assembly persists in a few niches.
For very large or very complex genomes, where adding any extra data source improves the result, some projects still incorporate Illumina data.
For metagenomics, where you're assembling many species at once from mixed samples, hybrid approaches sometimes help.
For some specialized applications where exquisite per-base accuracy is required, Illumina polishing of HiFi assemblies can still squeeze out marginal improvements.
But the era when hybrid was the standard, default approach is over.
The historical note matters because if you read papers from the 2015-2018 period describing genome assembly methodology, you'll see "PacBio plus Illumina" as the standard recipe.
That was right for that era.
The HiFi paradigm shift, in 2019-2020, made the second platform unnecessary for most cases.

It's a small thing, but it's part of the simplification that HiFi brought — one platform, one library prep, one sequencing run, one assembly — that has made high-quality genome assembly accessible to a much wider range of labs.
